Able, [Missing Link](/wiki/Missing_Link_Records "Missing Link Records"), [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records"), [True Tone](/wiki/True_Tone_Records_(Australian_label) "True Tone Records (Australian label)"), [Beggars Banquet](/wiki/Beggar%27s_Banquet_Records "Beggar's Banquet Records"), [Postcard](/wiki/Postcard_Records "Postcard Records"), [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") (US), [LO-MAX](/w/index.php?title=LO-MAX_Records&action=edit&redlink=1 "LO-MAX Records (page does not exist)")

were an Australian [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band formed in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), Queensland, in 1977. The band was co-founded and led by singer-songwriters and guitarists [Robert Forster](/wiki/Robert_Forster_(musician) "Robert Forster (musician)") and [Grant McLennan](/wiki/Grant_McLennan "Grant McLennan"), who were its only constant members throughout its existence. Drummer [Lindy Morrison](/wiki/Lindy_Morrison "Lindy Morrison") joined the band in 1980, and its lineup would later expand to include bass guitarist [Robert Vickers](/wiki/Robert_Vickers "Robert Vickers") and multi-instrumentalist [Amanda Brown](/wiki/Amanda_Brown_(musician) "Amanda Brown (musician)"). Vickers was replaced by [John Willsteed](/wiki/John_Willsteed "John Willsteed") in 1987, and the quintet lineup remained in place until the band split two years later. Forster and McLennan reformed the band in 2000 with a new lineup that did not include any previous personnel aside from them. McLennan died on 6 May 2006 of a heart attack and the Go-Betweens disbanded again. In 2010, a toll bridge in their native Brisbane was renamed the [Go Between Bridge](/wiki/Go_Between_Bridge "Go Between Bridge") after them.

**The Go-Betweens** were an Australian [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band formed in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), Queensland, in 1977. The band was co-founded and led by singer-songwriters and guitarists [Robert Forster](/wiki/Robert_Forster_(musician) "Robert Forster (musician)") and [Grant McLennan](/wiki/Grant_McLennan "Grant McLennan"), who were its only constant members throughout its existence. Drummer [Lindy Morrison](/wiki/Lindy_Morrison "Lindy Morrison") joined the band in 1980, and its lineup would later expand to include bass guitarist [Robert Vickers](/wiki/Robert_Vickers "Robert Vickers") and multi-instrumentalist [Amanda Brown](/wiki/Amanda_Brown_(musician) "Amanda Brown (musician)"). Vickers was replaced by [John Willsteed](/wiki/John_Willsteed "John Willsteed") in 1987, and the quintet lineup remained in place until the band split two years later. Forster and McLennan reformed the band in 2000 with a new lineup that did not include any previous personnel aside from them. McLennan died on 6 May 2006 of a heart attack and the Go-Betweens disbanded again. In 2010, a toll bridge in their native Brisbane was renamed the [Go Between Bridge](/wiki/Go_Between_Bridge "Go Between Bridge") after them.

In 1988, "[Streets of Your Town](/wiki/Streets_of_Your_Town "Streets of Your Town")", the first single from *[16 Lovers Lane](/wiki/16_Lovers_Lane "16 Lovers Lane")*, entered the Top 100 on both the [Kent Music Report](/wiki/Kent_Music_Report "Kent Music Report") chart in Australia and the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") in the United Kingdom. The follow-up single "[Was There Anything I Could Do?](/wiki/Was_There_Anything_I_Could_Do%3F "Was There Anything I Could Do?")" was a No. 16 hit on the *Billboard* [Modern Rock Tracks](/wiki/Alternative_Songs "Alternative Songs") chart in the United States. In May 2001, "[Cattle and Cane](/wiki/Cattle_and_Cane "Cattle and Cane")", from 1983's *[Before Hollywood](/wiki/Before_Hollywood "Before Hollywood")*, was selected by [Australasian Performing Right Association](/wiki/Australasian_Performing_Right_Association "Australasian Performing Right Association") (APRA) as one of the [Top 30 Australian songs](/wiki/APRA_Top_30_Australian_songs "APRA Top 30 Australian songs") of all time. In 2008, *16 Lovers Lane* was highlighted on [Special Broadcasting Service](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service") (SBS) TV's *The Great Australian Albums* series.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1977–1982: early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1977–1982: early years")]

[Robert Forster](/wiki/Robert_Forster_(musician) "Robert Forster (musician)") and [Grant McLennan](/wiki/Grant_McLennan "Grant McLennan") met at the [University of Queensland](/wiki/University_of_Queensland "University of Queensland"), where both were taking a theatre arts course.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) Forster provided vocals and guitar, while McLennan provided vocals, bass, and guitar; the pair shared songwriting duties. Together, they formed the Go-Betweens in December 1977 in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), Queensland. The name of the band reflects [L. P. Hartley](/wiki/L._P._Hartley "L. P. Hartley")'s classic novel *[The Go-Between](/wiki/The_Go-Between "The Go-Between")*.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) The band made its first public appearance as the [support act](/wiki/Opening_act "Opening act") for [the Numbers](/wiki/The_Riptides "The Riptides") at [Baroona Hall](/wiki/Baroona_Hall "Baroona Hall") in Brisbane in early April 1978.

> We performed two songs, and as soon as we got off stage, Mark Callaghan, Robert Vickers – we met them all, in five minutes ... They immediately asked us to play a second show.

— Robert Forster[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2)

The band, however, were still without a drummer, having borrowed Gerard Lee for their first show.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) They had a succession of drummers, starting with Bruce Anthon (ex–[the Survivors](/wiki/The_Survivors_(Australian_band) "The Survivors (Australian band)")). With a guest drummer, Dennis Cantwell ([the Riptides](/wiki/The_Riptides "The Riptides")), they recorded their debut single, "[Lee Remick](/wiki/Lee_Remick_(The_Go-Betweens_song) "Lee Remick (The Go-Betweens song)")", in May 1978. The song, an ode to the US actress [Lee Remick](/wiki/Lee_Remick "Lee Remick"), was released on the independent Able label in September 1978. The B-side to the single, "Karen", was a love song to a librarian. The sleeve depicts Forster and McLennan alongside portraits of [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Che Guevara](/wiki/Che_Guevara "Che Guevara") and [Lee Remick](/wiki/Lee_Remick "Lee Remick"). The band sent copies to record labels around the world, with interest shown by the UK arm of America's [Beserkley Records](/wiki/Beserkley_Records "Beserkley Records"). The group's first real drummer was Temucin "Tim" Mustafa, recruited after the recording of "Lee Remick", although he appears on the picture sleeve of the single.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) The band further expanded with the addition of guitarist [Peter Milton Walsh](/wiki/Peter_Milton_Walsh "Peter Milton Walsh"). Beserkley offered the band a contract that proposed the re-issue of "Lee Remick" and "Karen" as two singles, followed by an eight-album deal.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) The band recorded two more songs for Beserkley in November 1978 (including "The Sound of Rain"); however, when Beserkley went bust weeks later, Walsh left to form [the Apartments](/wiki/The_Apartments "The Apartments").[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4)

The band's second single, "People Say", which was recorded in May 1979, was produced by the Go-Betweens with Mustapha on drums and Malcolm Kelly on piano and organ. The B-side, "Don't Let Him Come Back", is a farewell to Walsh, who remained friends with Forster and McLennan.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) From May 1978 to May 1979, the group recorded some tracks live in Forster's bedroom using McLennan's two-track tape deck—they were not released until 1999 as *78 'Til 79 – The Lost Album*, which also includes both sides of the first two singles.[[5]](#cite_note-Swihart-5) These songs were simple pop tunes with a rough [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") edge, an obvious blend of pure pop influences such as [the Monkees](/wiki/The_Monkees "The Monkees") with the gritty simplicity of [the Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground").

In November 1979, the duo left Australia with a plan to shop their songs from record company to record company simply by visiting their offices and playing them.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) In [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland, on 28 April 1980, for independent label [Postcard](/wiki/Postcard_Records "Postcard Records"), they recorded their next single, "I Need Two Heads", with Steven Daly of [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") band [Orange Juice](/wiki/Orange_Juice_(band) "Orange Juice (band)") guesting on drums and [Alex Fergusson](/wiki/Alex_Fergusson_(musician) "Alex Fergusson (musician)") producing. Forster returned to Australia in June 1980, whilst McLennan continued to New York. They followed Australian contemporaries [the Birthday Party](/wiki/The_Birthday_Party_(band) "The Birthday Party (band)") to the busier music scene in London. "I Need Two Heads" peaked at No. 6 on the UK independent charts. Upon return to Brisbane they were joined by [Belinda "Lindy" Morrison](/wiki/Lindy_Morrison "Lindy Morrison") (ex-[Xero](/wiki/Xero_(band) "Xero (band)")) on drums in 1980. In November 1980 the band played their first [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") show at the [Paris Theatre](/wiki/Paris_Theatre,_Sydney "Paris Theatre, Sydney"), supporting the Birthday Party and the [Laughing Clowns](/wiki/Laughing_Clowns "Laughing Clowns").[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) The band impressed [Missing Link Records](/wiki/Missing_Link_Records "Missing Link Records") label boss, [Keith Glass](/wiki/Keith_Glass "Keith Glass"), which had re-issued "I Need Two Heads" for the Australian market, and offered to release the band's next single. Their fourth single, the first with Morrison, "Your Turn My Turn", was recorded in Sydney with [Tony Cohen](/wiki/Tony_Cohen "Tony Cohen") (the Birthday Party) in April 1981.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) The single was released in September.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[6]](#cite_note-ARDb-6) They recorded ten tracks as demos in Brisbane during 1981, which were released as *Very Quick on the Eye* by Man Made Records in 1982, the tracks showed that Morrison's "drum abilities, always a deeply underrated part of the band's appeal, fit hand in glove with the arrangements".[[7]](#cite_note-Raggett-7) By this time, Forster and Morrison were lovers and Morrison was living in [Spring Hill](/wiki/Spring_Hill,_Queensland "Spring Hill, Queensland"), a neighbourhood in Brisbane.[[8]](#cite_note-Dalton-8)

The band's first official album, *[Send Me a Lullaby](/wiki/Send_Me_a_Lullaby "Send Me a Lullaby")*, produced by the Go-Betweens and Tony Cohen, on Missing Link in Australia, was released as an eight-track mini-album in November 1981.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) Missing Link's UK distributors, [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records"), released the album in the UK, three months later, with four tracks added.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[6]](#cite_note-ARDb-6) Morrison provided the album title, in preference to *Two Wimps and a Witch*, from a [Zelda Fitzgerald](/wiki/Zelda_Fitzgerald "Zelda Fitzgerald") novel, *[Save Me the Waltz](/wiki/Save_Me_the_Waltz "Save Me the Waltz")*.[[9]](#cite_note-Kingsmill-9) The group had developed a subtler sound consisting of dry semi-spoken vocals, complex lyrics and melodic but fractious guitar pop influenced by contemporary bands such as [Television](/wiki/Television_(band) "Television (band)"), [Wire](/wiki/Wire_(band) "Wire (band)") and [Talking Heads](/wiki/Talking_Heads "Talking Heads"). Australian rock music historian [Ian McFarlane](/wiki/Ian_McFarlane "Ian McFarlane") described the album as "tentative and clumsy [with] its brittle, rough-hewn sound".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) Forster and McLennan wrote all the tracks, they alternated lead vocal duties, except "People Know" which had Morrison on vocals and [James Freud](/wiki/James_Freud "James Freud") ([Models](/wiki/Models_(band) "Models (band)")) guesting on saxophone.[[10]](#cite_note-Lullaby-10)[[11]](#cite_note-AllMLullaby-11) Enticed by Rough Trade, the band relocated to London.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) The band's next single, "Hammer the Hammer", was released by Rough Trade in July 1982.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[6]](#cite_note-ARDb-6) In 2002, UK label Circus released a 2×CD version of *Send Me a Lullaby* which included "After the Fireworks" recorded as a collaboration with the Birthday Party's [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave") on vocals, [Mick Harvey](/wiki/Mick_Harvey "Mick Harvey") on piano and [Rowland S. Howard](/wiki/Rowland_S._Howard "Rowland S. Howard") on guitar. It had been released as a single under the band name [Tuff Monks](/wiki/Tuff_Monks "Tuff Monks") in 1982 on [Au Go Go Records](/wiki/Au_Go_Go_Records "Au Go Go Records").[[12]](#cite_note-NicholsTuff-12)

### 1983–1989

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1983–1989")]

The Go-Betweens returned to the UK and recorded their second album, *[Before Hollywood](/wiki/Before_Hollywood "Before Hollywood")* (May 1983), with John Brand producing,[[6]](#cite_note-ARDb-6) at the International Christian Communications studio in [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne "Eastbourne").[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) It established them as cult favourites while "[Cattle and Cane](/wiki/Cattle_and_Cane "Cattle and Cane")" was released as a single and was "[a]rguably the band's absolute highlight of its earliest years".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[13]](#cite_note-AllMBefore-13) In Australia, the song had exposure on the national broadcaster [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") (ABC)'s iconic pop music TV series *[Countdown](/wiki/Countdown_(Australian_TV_series) "Countdown (Australian TV series)")*. Their tracks were played on ABC's alternative rock radio station [Triple J](/wiki/Triple_J "Triple J")—although mainly heard only in the Sydney region at that time. Despite the consistent critical acclaim their recordings garnered both in Australia and overseas, the Go-Betweens were mostly ignored by Australian commercial pop radio and never gained a broad national audience. *Before Hollywood* was described as "more world-weary ... full of deceptively simple yet accomplished songs".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)

[Robert Vickers](/wiki/Robert_Vickers "Robert Vickers") joined on bass guitar in late 1983—freeing McLennan for lead guitar work.[[2]](#cite_note-Pig-2) Their next album, *[Spring Hill Fair](/wiki/Spring_Hill_Fair "Spring Hill Fair")* (September 1984), was produced by Brand with Robert Andrews and Colin Fairley for [Sire Records](/wiki/Sire_Records "Sire Records").[[6]](#cite_note-ARDb-6) The album was acclaimed as "the sound was bolder and more confident", while "Man O' Sand to Girl O' Sea", "[Bachelor Kisses](/wiki/Bachelor_Kisses "Bachelor Kisses")" and "Part Company" were issued as singles.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) In 1985, the band signed with [True Tone Records](/wiki/True_Tone_Records_(Australian_label) "True Tone Records (Australian label)") distributed by [PolyGram](/wiki/PolyGram "PolyGram").[[6]](#cite_note-ARDb-6)

*[Liberty Belle and the Black Diamond Express](/wiki/Liberty_Belle_and_the_Black_Diamond_Express "Liberty Belle and the Black Diamond Express")*, released in March 1986 on [Beggars Banquet Records](/wiki/Beggars_Banquet_Records "Beggars Banquet Records"), received favourable reviews, and showed the band gradually moving towards a smoother and more contemporary sound while retaining elements of their idiosyncratic early style.[[14]](#cite_note-AllMLiberty-14) McFarlane claims "[it] remains the band's most cohesive and finely crafted statement".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) "[Spring Rain](/wiki/Spring_Rain_(Go-Betweens_song) "Spring Rain (Go-Betweens song)")" (February) and "[Head Full of Steam](/wiki/Head_Full_of_Steam "Head Full of Steam")" (June) were released as singles, with "Spring Rain" reaching the Australian [Kent Music Report](/wiki/Kent_Music_Report "Kent Music Report") Singles Chart Top 100.[[15]](#cite_note-Kent-15)

[Amanda Brown](/wiki/Amanda_Brown_(musician) "Amanda Brown (musician)") on violin, oboe, guitar, keyboards and backing vocals joined later in the year. Within a few months, Brown and McLennan were lovers—many of McLennan's new lyrics were about this relationship.[[9]](#cite_note-Kingsmill-9)[[16]](#cite_note-Beriyant-16) *[Tallulah](/wiki/Tallulah_(The_Go-Betweens_album) "Tallulah (The Go-Betweens album)")* (June 1987), produced by the Go-Betweens for True Tone and Beggars Banquet, contained their "most winsome and hummable songs, '[Right Here](/wiki/Right_Here_(The_Go-Betweens_song) "Right Here (The Go-Betweens song)")' and '[Bye Bye Pride](/wiki/Bye_Bye_Pride "Bye Bye Pride")'"; while Brown's contributions "added extra lustre".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) [LO-MAX Records](/w/index.php?title=LO-MAX_Records&action=edit&redlink=1 "LO-MAX Records (page does not exist)") released a 2×CD version of *Tallulah* in 2004; one of the additional tracks, "Doo Wop in 'A' (Bam Boom)", was co-written by Morrison, Brown, McLennan and Forster.[[17]](#cite_note-APRA_DooWop-17) In November 1987, the Go-Betweens returned to Australia and [John Willsteed](/wiki/John_Willsteed "John Willsteed") (ex-Xero with Morrison) replaced Vickers on bass.

*[16 Lovers Lane](/wiki/16_Lovers_Lane "16 Lovers Lane")* (1988) was the group's most commercial offering, providing the alternative radio hit "[Streets of Your Town](/wiki/Streets_of_Your_Town "Streets of Your Town")" (1988), which entered the singles charts in both the UK and Australia peaking in the Top 100 but not higher than the number 80. The follow-up single "[Was There Anything I Could Do?](/wiki/Was_There_Anything_I_Could_Do%3F "Was There Anything I Could Do?")" was a No. 16 hit on US alternative Modern Rock radio stations, and Beggars Banquet, trying to encourage the band's commercial momentum, re-released "Streets of Your Town" in the UK in early 1989, where it charted low once again. These minimal successes were hardly the hoped-for commercial breakthrough for the band.

After recording six albums, Forster and McLennan decided in December 1989 to disband the Go-Betweens. There were tentative plans to form an acoustic duo together. When McLennan told Brown, she ended their relationship.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Forster and McLennan pursued solo careers. Brown and Morrison formed Cleopatra Wong in 1991.

All official albums released in the 1980s have titles with a double L word, except *16 Lovers Lane*, which has two words beginning with an L. For their first few albums, this was simply a coincidence; by the end of the decade, for their own amusement the band deliberately kept to a self-imposed 'tradition' of album titles that featured a double L.

### 2000–2006: reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000–2006: reformation")]

Forster and McLennan pursued solo careers throughout the 1990s, and McLennan also collaborated with [Steve Kilbey](/wiki/Steve_Kilbey "Steve Kilbey") of [The Church](/wiki/The_Church_(band) "The Church (band)") in the studio project band [Jack Frost](/wiki/Jack_Frost_(Australian_band) "Jack Frost (Australian band)").

Forster and McLennan were inspired to work together again after they were invited by fans at French music magazine *[Les Inrockuptibles](/wiki/Les_Inrockuptibles "Les Inrockuptibles")* to perform at the magazine's 10th anniversary on 23 May 1996 in Paris.[[19]](#cite_note-Les_Inrocks-19) For this performance the band comprised Forster, McLennan, Adele Pickvance on bass guitar and [Glenn Thompson](/wiki/Glenn_Thompson_(musician) "Glenn Thompson (musician)") on drums.[[20]](#cite_note-Simon_McKenzie-20)

In 2000, Forster, McLennan and Pickvance went to Jackpot! studio in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), with members of [Sleater-Kinney](/wiki/Sleater-Kinney "Sleater-Kinney"), and recorded the album *[The Friends of Rachel Worth](/wiki/The_Friends_of_Rachel_Worth "The Friends of Rachel Worth")* (the first album to ignore their "double L" titling tradition).

In 2001 Thompson rejoined the band for the Australian [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out") Festival. This line up of Forster, McLennan, Pickvance and Thompson went on to record *[Bright Yellow Bright Orange](/wiki/Bright_Yellow_Bright_Orange "Bright Yellow Bright Orange")* and in October 2005, the Go-Betweens finally achieved mainstream recognition, with the album *[Oceans Apart](/wiki/Oceans_Apart "Oceans Apart")* (produced by Mark Wallis and Dave Ruffy) winning the [ARIA Award for Best Adult Contemporary Album](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Adult_Contemporary_Album "ARIA Award for Best Adult Contemporary Album"). Grant McLennan died on 6 May 2006 of a heart attack, and Robert Forster subsequently announced that the Go-Betweens were no more.[[21]](#cite_note-Christgau-21) Forster has continued to perform and records as a solo artist and has also written well-received music criticism.

In August 2006, several of their albums re-entered the ARIA top 500.[[22]](#cite_note-ARIA_history_pages-22)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Go_between_bridge.jpg/250px-Go_between_bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Go_between_bridge.jpg)

Opened in 2010, the [Go Between Bridge](/wiki/Go_Between_Bridge "Go Between Bridge") in Brisbane is named after the band.

The focal point of the Go-Betweens was the song writing skills of Forster and McLennan, described by *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")* critic [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") as "the greatest songwriting partnership working today." Each developed a distinctive but complementary style: Forster's songs were angular and angst-ridden, making much use of irony and unusual lyrical imagery, while McLennan's were generally softer and more sensitive, his lyrics often based on character study and reported speech. They never secured an Australian or UK Top 50 chart single—a fact which mystified their supporters in the press, to the point where this "scandalous" lack of popular success became a cliché when writing about the band. However, there were a few very minor chart successes for the band, starting with "Spring Rain", which snuck into the lower rungs of the Australian charts in 1986 and became the band's first ever Top 100 chart hit. The following year, "[Right Here](/wiki/Right_Here_(The_Go-Betweens_song) "Right Here (The Go-Betweens song)")" similarly rode low on the UK Top 100 Charts. Then in 1988, "Streets of Your Town", the first single from 1988's *16 Lovers Lane*, reached the Top 100 in both Australia and UK.

In May 2001 "Cattle and Cane", written by McLennan and Forster,[[23]](#cite_note-APRA_Cattle-23) was selected by [Australasian Performing Right Association](/wiki/Australasian_Performing_Right_Association "Australasian Performing Right Association") (APRA) as one of the [Top 30 Australian songs](/wiki/APRA_Top_30_Australian_songs "APRA Top 30 Australian songs") of all time.[[24]](#cite_note-Kruger-24) McLennan described writing the lyrics:

> I wrote (the song) to please my mother. She hasn't heard it yet because my mother and stepfather live (on a cattle station) and they can't get 240 volts electricity there, so I have to sing it over the phone to her ... I don't like the word nostalgic; to me, it's a sloppy yearning for the past, and I'm not trying to do that in that song. I'm just trying to put three vignettes of a person, who's a lot like myself, growing up in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), and just juxtaposing that against how I am now.[[25]](#cite_note-Jeff-25)

— Grant McLennan, 1983

Their song, "Streets of Your Town", was used by [Prime Television](/wiki/Prime_Television "Prime Television") and [GWN](/wiki/Golden_West_Network "Golden West Network") in their station identification from 2001 to 2003. Elements from the same song were sampled in the 2003 single "[Just The Way You Are](/wiki/Just_The_Way_You_Are_(Milky_song) "Just The Way You Are (Milky song)")" by the Italian dance group [Milky](/wiki/Milky "Milky").

In the [4th Season](/wiki/24_(season_4) "24 (season 4)") of American TV Series *[24](/wiki/24_(TV_series) "24 (TV series)")* a company is named McLennan-Forster—the producer of the series and author, [Evan Katz](/wiki/Evan_Katz "Evan Katz"), wanted to express his veneration of the Go-Betweens by this company name.[[26]](#cite_note-Snierson-26)

On the eve of the first anniversary of McLennan's death, [Triple J](/wiki/Triple_J "Triple J") & [JTV](/wiki/Triple_j_tv "Triple j tv") broadcast a tribute concert to The Go-Betweens, recorded in 2006 at Brisbane venue, the [Tivoli Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Tivoli_Theatre_(Brisbane)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tivoli Theatre (Brisbane) (page does not exist)"). Inspired by this tribute concert, and featuring many of the same artists, a tribute album to the Go-Betweens, *[Write Your Adventures Down](/w/index.php?title=Write_Your_Adventures_Down&action=edit&redlink=1 "Write Your Adventures Down (page does not exist)")*, was released in June 2007 in Australia by The Red Label.

In 2008, *16 Lovers Lane* was highlighted on [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service") TV's *[Great Australian Albums](/wiki/Great_Australian_Albums "Great Australian Albums")* series as a classic example of 1980s rock music.[[27]](#cite_note-SBSGreat-27)
The documentary was later released on DVD.

On 29 September 2009, [Brisbane City Council](/wiki/Brisbane_City_Council "Brisbane City Council") announced that a four-lane traffic bridge, previously known as Hale Street Link, would be renamed as [Go Between Bridge](/wiki/Go_Between_Bridge "Go Between Bridge") in the band's honour, following a popularity poll.[[28]](#cite_note-Pollard-28)

The Go-Betweens are referenced in the [Teenage Fanclub](/wiki/Teenage_Fanclub "Teenage Fanclub") song "When I Still Have Thee" (2010), the [Belle and Sebastian](/wiki/Belle_and_Sebastian "Belle and Sebastian") song "Shoot the Sexual Athlete" (2001), the song "Don't Want to Be Grant McLennan" (1991) by fellow Australians [Smudge](/wiki/Smudge_(band) "Smudge (band)"), and the song "Kathy" (1993) by [Clouds](/wiki/Clouds_(Australian_band) "Clouds (Australian band)").

[Nada Surf](/wiki/Nada_Surf "Nada Surf") covered their song "Love Goes On!" on their covers album *[If I Had a Hi-Fi](/wiki/If_I_Had_a_Hi-Fi "If I Had a Hi-Fi")* (2010).

Robert Forster continues to perform and also contribute articles to *[The Monthly](/wiki/The_Monthly "The Monthly")* magazine. On the 3rd February 2023 he released his eighth studio album, *[The Candle and the Flame](/wiki/The_Candle_and_the_Flame "The Candle and the Flame")* featuring Adele Pickvance on bass.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Many Australian bands and artists associated with the [dolewave](/wiki/Dolewave "Dolewave") genre cite the Go-Betweens as a major influence, including [Dick Diver](/wiki/Dick_Diver "Dick Diver") and singer-songwriter [Courtney Barnett](/wiki/Courtney_Barnett "Courtney Barnett").[[30]](#cite_note-ev-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Awards and nominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Awards and nominations")]

### ARIA Music Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: ARIA Music Awards")]

The [ARIA Music Awards](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards "ARIA Music Awards") is an annual awards ceremony that recognises excellence, innovation, and achievement across all genres of [Australian music](/wiki/Australian_music "Australian music"). They commenced in 1987. The Go-Betweens won one award.

| Year | Nominee / work | Award | Result | Ref. |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [1989](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_1989 "ARIA Music Awards of 1989") | *16 Lovers Lane* | [Best Group](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Group "ARIA Award for Best Group") | Nominated | [[40]](#cite_note-ARIA1989-40) |
| "Streets of Your Town" | [Song of the Year](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "ARIA Award for Song of the Year") | Nominated |
| [2003](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_2003 "ARIA Music Awards of 2003") | *Bright Yellow Bright Orange* | [Best Adult Contemporary Album](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Adult_Contemporary_Album "ARIA Award for Best Adult Contemporary Album") | Nominated | [[41]](#cite_note-ARIA2003-41) |
| [2005](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_2005 "ARIA Music Awards of 2005") | *Oceans Apart* | Best Adult Contemporary Album | Won | [[42]](#cite_note-ARIA2005-42) |
| [2006](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_2006 "ARIA Music Awards of 2006") | *That Striped Sunlight Sound* | [Best Music DVD](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Music_DVD "ARIA Award for Best Music DVD") | Nominated | [[43]](#cite_note-ARIA2006-43) |

### Australian Music Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Go-Betweens&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Australian Music Prize")]

The [Australian Music Prize](/wiki/Australian_Music_Prize "Australian Music Prize") (the AMP) is an annual award of $30,000 given to an Australian band or solo artist in recognition of the merit of an album released during the year of award. They commenced in 2005.

| Year | Nominee / work | Award | Result |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2005[[44]](#cite_note-44) | *Oceans Apart* | Australian Music Prize | Nominated |
